revision.text:
[[ja:佐志能神社 (笠間市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q28684144]] Wikidata name: Sashinō Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[Category:pages with merged translations]] ==Merged second translation== {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Sashino Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Sashino jinja shrine in Kasama city.jpg |location = [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]], [[:en:Kasama, Ibaraki|Kasama City]], Kasama 3613 |LatitudeDeg = 36 |LatitudeMin = 22 |LatitudeSec = 56.98 |LongitudeDeg= 140 |LongitudeMin= 16 |LongitudeSec= 2.89 |Map = Japan Ibaraki |Deities = {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}
[[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Kami]]
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}} |SacredObject= |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} candidate
Former [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Village Shrine]] |Established = Unknown |MainHall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage = |Festival = November 16 |Rituals = |prefecture= {{ill|Kasama|WD=Q845851}} |coordinates= {{Coord|36.3824944|N|140.2674694|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deity= {{ill|Prince Toyokiirihiko|WD=Q11633375}}; {{ill|Takemikazuchi|WD=Q1502690}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}} }} '''Sashino Shrine''' (佐志能神社, ''Sashinō Jinja'') is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kasama City]], [[:en:Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]], situated on [[Sashirō Mountain]], which includes the ruins of [[:en:Kasama Castle|Kasama Castle]]. It is a candidate for the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} listed in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', classified as a small shrine in [[:en:Hitachi Province|Hitachi Province]]'s [[Niihari District]]. The shrine was previously Ranked as a village shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. Due to damage caused by the [[:en:2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the Kasama Castle main keep area, including Sashino Shrine, is listed as off-limits on Kasama City's official website.[http://www.city.kasama.lg.jp/page/page000776.html Kasama Castle Site], Kasama City official website. However, practical access is possible, although fallen trees from Typhoon No. 19 in October 2019 have made the parking area inaccessible. == Overview == The shrine is located at the summit of Sashirō Mountain (182.1 meters), at the ruins of the main keep of Kasama Castle, designated as a tangible cultural property by Kasama City. It is part of the Kasama Prefectural Natural Park (Castle Ruins Park). The mountain is both a historical castle site and a scenic spot, included in the "100 Scenic Spots of Ibaraki" and the "100 Forest Bathing Paths of Ibaraki" under the theme "Kasama Inari and Sashirō Mountain." Sashirō Mountain has also been known as "Sanpaku Mountain," "Sajō Mountain," and "Sashinō Mountain." Locally, it is referred to as "Castle Mountain" due to its association with the castle. The ''[[Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|Hitachi Fudoki]]'' mentions it as a "great mountain" located on the border between [[Niihari District (Hitachi Province)|Niihari District]] and [[Naka District (Hitachi Province)|Naka District]]. There are various theories regarding the origin of its name: * According to the tradition of {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|ja|正福寺_(笠間市)|de|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|1=Shōfuku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630500}}, the mountain's divine messengers— "white horse, white deer, and white pheasant"—led to the name "Sanpaku" (Three Whites). The Kasama City explanation states these messengers are a "white pheasant, white deer, and white fox." * The ''New Hitachi Provincial Records'' interpret the ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'' passage referring to the descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and connect it to the etymology of Sashino. It suggests "Sashinō" evolved through phonetic shifts, implying the name originated as "Sajino," which later transformed to "Sashinō." The mountain summit, separate from the former main keep, is also referred to as "Abu Mountain." The shrine once bore alternate names such as "Sanpaku Gongen" and "Abu Gongen," reflecting its historical ties to the mountain. == Deities == ;Principal deities: * {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Kami]] * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}} ;Enshrined together: * Hachiman Shrine ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|品陀和気命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|1=Ōjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q317997}}) * Shiroyama Inari Shrine ({{ill|Uka-no-Mitama|ja|宇迦之御魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uka-no-Mitama|1=Ukanomitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080728}}) * Itsukishima Shrine ({{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵志比賣命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}) * Kagutsuchi Shrine ({{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|ja|軻遇突智神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}) * Success Inari Shrine (Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami) The shrine's deities were finalized during its relocation to the summit of Sashirō Mountain in 1873. In 1874, five shrines, including Hachiman Shrine and Shiroyama Inari Shrine, were consolidated. == Festivals == The main festival is held on November 16, along with the February 15 ''Kinen-sai'' (Prayer Festival) and the April 22 ''Rinji-sai'' (Special Festival). Sashino Shrine is managed by [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], with its events listed on their website. == References == {{Reflist|refs= The Ibaraki Prefectural Shrine Registry lists the Sashino Shrines in [[Sakuranome]] and [[Murakami]] as "Sashino," while the one in Kasama City is listed as "Sashinō." [http://www.sasiro.jp/info/index.html History of Sashirō Mountain Shōfuku-ji], retrieved September 10, 2016. [http://www.city.kasama.lg.jp/page/page006719.html History of Kasama Castle], Kasama City official website, retrieved September 10, 2016. [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ National Diet Library Digital Collections], citing ''Greater Japan Geographic Dictionary.'' Ibaraki Prefecture, [http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/seikatsukankyo/kansei/shizen/koen/09.html Kasama Prefectural Natural Park], retrieved September 10, 2016. }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Son-sha in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Kasama City]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kasama]] [[Category:100 Views of Ibaraki]] }} [[ja:佐志能神社 (笠間市)]] {{translated page|ja|佐志能神社 (笠間市)|version=101503126|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{wikidata link|Q28684144}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

==Merged second translation== {{Shrine |name = Sashino Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Sashino jinja shrine in Kasama city.jpg|280px]] |location = [[Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]], [[Kasama, Ibaraki|Kasama City]], Kasama 3613 |LatitudeDeg = 36 |LatitudeMin = 22 |LatitudeSec = 56.98 |LongitudeDeg= 140 |LongitudeMin= 16 |LongitudeSec= 2.89 |Map = Japan Ibaraki |Deities = {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}
[[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Kami]]
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}} |SacredObject= |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} candidate
Former [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Village Shrine]] |Established = Unknown |MainHall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage = |Festival = November 16 |Rituals = }} '''Sashino Shrine''' (佐志能神社, ''Sashinō Jinja'') is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kasama City]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]], situated on [[Sashirō Mountain]], which includes the ruins of [[Kasama Castle]]. It is a candidate for the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} listed in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', classified as a small shrine in [[Hitachi Province]]'s [[Niihari District]]. The shrine was previously Ranked as a village shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. Due to damage caused by the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the Kasama Castle main keep area, including Sashino Shrine, is listed as off-limits on Kasama City's official website.[http://www.city.kasama.lg.jp/page/page000776.html Kasama Castle Site], Kasama City official website. However, practical access is possible, although fallen trees from Typhoon No. 19 in October 2019 have made the parking area inaccessible. == Overview == The shrine is located at the summit of Sashirō Mountain (182.1 meters), at the ruins of the main keep of Kasama Castle, designated as a tangible cultural property by Kasama City. It is part of the Kasama Prefectural Natural Park (Castle Ruins Park). The mountain is both a historical castle site and a scenic spot, included in the "100 Scenic Spots of Ibaraki" and the "100 Forest Bathing Paths of Ibaraki" under the theme "Kasama Inari and Sashirō Mountain." Sashirō Mountain has also been known as "Sanpaku Mountain," "Sajō Mountain," and "Sashinō Mountain." Locally, it is referred to as "Castle Mountain" due to its association with the castle. The ''[[Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|Hitachi Fudoki]]'' mentions it as a "great mountain" located on the border between [[Niihari District (Hitachi Province)|Niihari District]] and [[Naka District (Hitachi Province)|Naka District]]. There are various theories regarding the origin of its name: * According to the tradition of {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|ja|正福寺_(笠間市)|de|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|qq=}}, the mountain's divine messengers— "white horse, white deer, and white pheasant"—led to the name "Sanpaku" (Three Whites). The Kasama City explanation states these messengers are a "white pheasant, white deer, and white fox." * The ''New Hitachi Provincial Records'' interpret the ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'' passage referring to the descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and connect it to the etymology of Sashino. It suggests "Sashinō" evolved through phonetic shifts, implying the name originated as "Sajino," which later transformed to "Sashinō." The mountain summit, separate from the former main keep, is also referred to as "Abu Mountain." The shrine once bore alternate names such as "Sanpaku Gongen" and "Abu Gongen," reflecting its historical ties to the mountain. == Deities == ;Principal deities: * {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Kami]] * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}} ;Enshrined together: * Hachiman Shrine ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|品陀和気命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|qq=}}) * Shiroyama Inari Shrine ({{ill|Uka-no-Mitama|ja|宇迦之御魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uka-no-Mitama|qq=}}) * Itsukishima Shrine ({{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵志比賣命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}) * Kagutsuchi Shrine ({{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|ja|軻遇突智神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}) * Success Inari Shrine (Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami) The shrine's deities were finalized during its relocation to the summit of Sashirō Mountain in 1873. In 1874, five shrines, including Hachiman Shrine and Shiroyama Inari Shrine, were consolidated. == Festivals == The main festival is held on November 16, along with the February 15 ''Kinen-sai'' (Prayer Festival) and the April 22 ''Rinji-sai'' (Special Festival). Sashino Shrine is managed by [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], with its events listed on their website. == References == {{Reflist|refs= The Ibaraki Prefectural Shrine Registry lists the Sashino Shrines in [[Sakuranome]] and [[Murakami]] as "Sashino," while the one in Kasama City is listed as "Sashinō." [http://www.sasiro.jp/info/index.html History of Sashirō Mountain Shōfuku-ji], retrieved September 10, 2016. [http://www.city.kasama.lg.jp/page/page006719.html History of Kasama Castle], Kasama City official website, retrieved September 10, 2016. [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ National Diet Library Digital Collections], citing ''Greater Japan Geographic Dictionary.'' Ibaraki Prefecture, [http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/seikatsukankyo/kansei/shizen/koen/09.html Kasama Prefectural Natural Park], retrieved September 10, 2016. }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:Historic sites in Kasama City]] [[Category:100 Scenic Spots of Ibaraki]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:pages that were shrank]] [[sn:Sashino Shrine (Kasama)]]}} [[ja:佐志能神社 (笠間市)]]

The mountain is both a historical castle site and a scenic spot, included in the "100 Scenic Spots of Ibaraki" and the "100 Forest Bathing Paths of Ibaraki" under the theme "Kasama Inari and Sashirō Mountain." Sashirō Mountain has also been known as "Sanpaku Mountain," "Sajō Mountain," and "Sashinō Mountain." Locally, it is referred to as "Castle Mountain" due to its association with the castle. The ''[[Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|Hitachi Fudoki]]'' mentions it as a "great mountain" located on the border between [[Niihari District (Hitachi Province)|Niihari District]] and [[Naka District (Hitachi Province)|Naka District]]. There are various theories regarding the origin of its name: * According to the tradition of {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|ja|正福寺_(笠間市)|de|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|qq=}}, the mountain's divine messengers— "white horse, white deer, and white pheasant"—led to the name "Sanpaku" (Three Whites). The Kasama City explanation states these messengers are a "white pheasant, white deer, and white fox." * The ''New Hitachi Provincial Records'' interpret the ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'' passage referring to the descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and connect it to the etymology of Sashino. It suggests "Sashinō" evolved through phonetic shifts, implying the name originated as "Sajino," which later transformed to "Sashinō." The mountain summit, separate from the former main keep, is also referred to as "Abu Mountain." The shrine once bore alternate names such as "Sanpaku Gongen" and "Abu Gongen," reflecting its historical ties to the mountain. == Deities == ;Principal deities: * {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Kami]] * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}} ;Enshrined together: * Hachiman Shrine ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|品陀和気命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|qq=}}) * Shiroyama Inari Shrine ({{ill|Uka-no-Mitama|ja|宇迦之御魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uka-no-Mitama|qq=}}) * Itsukishima Shrine ({{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵志比賣命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}) * Kagutsuchi Shrine ({{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|ja|軻遇突智神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}) * Success Inari Shrine (Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami) The shrine's deities were finalized during its relocation to the summit of Sashirō Mountain in 1873. In 1874, five shrines, including Hachiman Shrine and Shiroyama Inari Shrine, were consolidated. == Festivals == The main festival is held on November 16, along with the February 15 ''Kinen-sai'' (Prayer Festival) and the April 22 ''Rinji-sai'' (Special Festival). Sashino Shrine is managed by [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], with its events listed on their website. == References == {{Reflist|refs= The Ibaraki Prefectural Shrine Registry lists the Sashino Shrines in [[Sakuranome]] and [[Murakami]] as "Sashino," while the one in Kasama City is listed as "Sashinō." [http://www.sasiro.jp/info/index.html History of Sashirō Mountain Shōfuku-ji], retrieved September 10, 2016. [http://www.city.kasama.lg.jp/page/page006719.html History of Kasama Castle], Kasama City official website, retrieved September 10, 2016. [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ National Diet Library Digital Collections], citing ''Greater Japan Geographic Dictionary.'' Ibaraki Prefecture, [http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/seikatsukankyo/kansei/shizen/koen/09.html Kasama Prefectural Natural Park], retrieved September 10, 2016. }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:Historic sites in Kasama City]] [[Category:100 Scenic Spots of Ibaraki]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:pages that were shrank]] [[sn:Sashino Shrine (Kasama)]]}} [[ja:佐志能神社 (笠間市)]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

Due to damage caused by the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the Kasama Castle main keep area, including Sashino Shrine, is listed as off-limits on Kasama City's official website.[http://www.city.kasama.lg.jp/page/page000776.html Kasama Castle Site], Kasama City official website. However, practical access is possible, although fallen trees from Typhoon No. 19 in October 2019 have made the parking area inaccessible. == Overview == The shrine is located at the summit of Sashirō Mountain (182.1 meters), at the ruins of the main keep of Kasama Castle, designated as a tangible cultural property by Kasama City. It is part of the Kasama Prefectural Natural Park (Castle Ruins Park). The mountain is both a historical castle site and a scenic spot, included in the "100 Scenic Spots of Ibaraki" and the "100 Forest Bathing Paths of Ibaraki" under the theme "Kasama Inari and Sashirō Mountain." Sashirō Mountain has also been known as "Sanpaku Mountain," "Sajō Mountain," and "Sashinō Mountain." Locally, it is referred to as "Castle Mountain" due to its association with the castle. The ''[[Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|Hitachi Fudoki]]'' mentions it as a "great mountain" located on the border between [[Niihari District (Hitachi Province)|Niihari District]] and [[Naka District (Hitachi Province)|Naka District]]. There are various theories regarding the origin of its name: * According to the tradition of {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|ja|正福寺_(笠間市)|de|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|qq=}}, the mountain's divine messengers— "white horse, white deer, and white pheasant"—led to the name "Sanpaku" (Three Whites). The Kasama City explanation states these messengers are a "white pheasant, white deer, and white fox." * The ''New Hitachi Provincial Records'' interpret the ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'' passage referring to the descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and connect it to the etymology of Sashino. It suggests "Sashinō" evolved through phonetic shifts, implying the name originated as "Sajino," which later transformed to "Sashinō." The mountain summit, separate from the former main keep, is also referred to as "Abu Mountain." The shrine once bore alternate names such as "Sanpaku Gongen" and "Abu Gongen," reflecting its historical ties to the mountain. == Deities == ;Principal deities: * {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Kami]] * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}} ;Enshrined together: * Hachiman Shrine ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|品陀和気命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|qq=}}) * Shiroyama Inari Shrine ({{ill|Uka-no-Mitama|ja|宇迦之御魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uka-no-Mitama|qq=}}) * Itsukishima Shrine ({{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵志比賣命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}) * Kagutsuchi Shrine ({{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|ja|軻遇突智神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}) * Success Inari Shrine (Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami) The shrine's deities were finalized during its relocation to the summit of Sashirō Mountain in 1873. In 1874, five shrines, including Hachiman Shrine and Shiroyama Inari Shrine, were consolidated. == Festivals == The main festival is held on November 16, along with the February 15 ''Kinen-sai'' (Prayer Festival) and the April 22 ''Rinji-sai'' (Special Festival). Sashino Shrine is managed by [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], with its events listed on their website. == References == {{Reflist|refs= The Ibaraki Prefectural Shrine Registry lists the Sashino Shrines in [[Sakuranome]] and [[Murakami]] as "Sashino," while the one in Kasama City is listed as "Sashinō." [http://www.sasiro.jp/info/index.html History of Sashirō Mountain Shōfuku-ji], retrieved September 10, 2016. [http://www.city.kasama.lg.jp/page/page006719.html History of Kasama Castle], Kasama City official website, retrieved September 10, 2016. [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ National Diet Library Digital Collections], citing ''Greater Japan Geographic Dictionary.'' Ibaraki Prefecture, [http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/seikatsukankyo/kansei/shizen/koen/09.html Kasama Prefectural Natural Park], retrieved September 10, 2016. }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:Historic sites in Kasama City]] [[Category:100 Scenic Spots of Ibaraki]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:pages that were shrank]] [[sn:Sashino Shrine (Kasama)]]}} [[ja:佐志能神社 (笠間市)]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

Former [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Village Shrine]] |Established = Unknown |MainHall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage = |Festival = November 16 |Rituals = }} '''Sashino Shrine''' (佐志能神社, ''Sashinō Jinja'') is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kasama City]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]], situated on [[Sashirō Mountain]], which includes the ruins of [[Kasama Castle]]. It is a candidate for the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} listed in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', classified as a small shrine in [[Hitachi Province]]'s [[Niihari District]]. The shrine was previously Ranked as a village shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. Due to damage caused by the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the Kasama Castle main keep area, including Sashino Shrine, is listed as off-limits on Kasama City's official website.[http://www.city.kasama.lg.jp/page/page000776.html Kasama Castle Site], Kasama City official website. However, practical access is possible, although fallen trees from Typhoon No. 19 in October 2019 have made the parking area inaccessible. == Overview == The shrine is located at the summit of Sashirō Mountain (182.1 meters), at the ruins of the main keep of Kasama Castle, designated as a tangible cultural property by Kasama City. It is part of the Kasama Prefectural Natural Park (Castle Ruins Park). The mountain is both a historical castle site and a scenic spot, included in the "100 Scenic Spots of Ibaraki" and the "100 Forest Bathing Paths of Ibaraki" under the theme "Kasama Inari and Sashirō Mountain." Sashirō Mountain has also been known as "Sanpaku Mountain," "Sajō Mountain," and "Sashinō Mountain." Locally, it is referred to as "Castle Mountain" due to its association with the castle. The ''[[Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|Hitachi Fudoki]]'' mentions it as a "great mountain" located on the border between [[Niihari District (Hitachi Province)|Niihari District]] and [[Naka District (Hitachi Province)|Naka District]]. There are various theories regarding the origin of its name: * According to the tradition of {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|ja|正福寺_(笠間市)|de|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|qq=}}, the mountain's divine messengers— "white horse, white deer, and white pheasant"—led to the name "Sanpaku" (Three Whites). The Kasama City explanation states these messengers are a "white pheasant, white deer, and white fox." * The ''New Hitachi Provincial Records'' interpret the ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'' passage referring to the descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and connect it to the etymology of Sashino. It suggests "Sashinō" evolved through phonetic shifts, implying the name originated as "Sajino," which later transformed to "Sashinō." The mountain summit, separate from the former main keep, is also referred to as "Abu Mountain." The shrine once bore alternate names such as "Sanpaku Gongen" and "Abu Gongen," reflecting its historical ties to the mountain. == Deities == ;Principal deities: * {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Kami]] * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}} ;Enshrined together: * Hachiman Shrine ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|品陀和気命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|qq=}}) * Shiroyama Inari Shrine ({{ill|Uka-no-Mitama|ja|宇迦之御魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uka-no-Mitama|qq=}}) * Itsukishima Shrine ({{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵志比賣命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}) * Kagutsuchi Shrine ({{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|ja|軻遇突智神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}) * Success Inari Shrine (Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami) The shrine's deities were finalized during its relocation to the summit of Sashirō Mountain in 1873. In 1874, five shrines, including Hachiman Shrine and Shiroyama Inari Shrine, were consolidated. == Festivals == The main festival is held on November 16, along with the February 15 ''Kinen-sai'' (Prayer Festival) and the April 22 ''Rinji-sai'' (Special Festival). Sashino Shrine is managed by [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], with its events listed on their website. == References == {{Reflist|refs= The Ibaraki Prefectural Shrine Registry lists the Sashino Shrines in [[Sakuranome]] and [[Murakami]] as "Sashino," while the one in Kasama City is listed as "Sashinō." [http://www.sasiro.jp/info/index.html History of Sashirō Mountain Shōfuku-ji], retrieved September 10, 2016. [http://www.city.kasama.lg.jp/page/page006719.html History of Kasama Castle], Kasama City official website, retrieved September 10, 2016. [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ National Diet Library Digital Collections], citing ''Greater Japan Geographic Dictionary.'' Ibaraki Prefecture, [http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/seikatsukankyo/kansei/shizen/koen/09.html Kasama Prefectural Natural Park], retrieved September 10, 2016. }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:Historic sites in Kasama City]] [[Category:100 Scenic Spots of Ibaraki]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:pages that were shrank]] [[sn:Sashino Shrine (Kasama)]]}} [[ja:佐志能神社 (笠間市)]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] == Accidentally Overwritten Content == [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] ==Merged second translation== {{Shrine |name = Sashino Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Sashino jinja shrine in Kasama city.jpg|280px]] |location = [[Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]], [[Kasama, Ibaraki|Kasama City]], Kasama 3613 |LatitudeDeg = 36 |LatitudeMin = 22 |LatitudeSec = 56.98 |LongitudeDeg= 140 |LongitudeMin= 16 |LongitudeSec= 2.89 |Map = Japan Ibaraki |Deities = {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}

Due to damage caused by the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the Kasama Castle main keep area, including Sashino Shrine, is listed as off-limits on Kasama City's official website.[http://www.city.kasama.lg.jp/page/page000776.html Kasama Castle Site], Kasama City official website. However, practical access is possible, although fallen trees from Typhoon No. 19 in October 2019 have made the parking area inaccessible. == Overview == The shrine is located at the summit of Sashirō Mountain (182.1 meters), at the ruins of the main keep of Kasama Castle, designated as a tangible cultural property by Kasama City. It is part of the Kasama Prefectural Natural Park (Castle Ruins Park). The mountain is both a historical castle site and a scenic spot, included in the "100 Scenic Spots of Ibaraki" and the "100 Forest Bathing Paths of Ibaraki" under the theme "Kasama Inari and Sashirō Mountain." Sashirō Mountain has also been known as "Sanpaku Mountain," "Sajō Mountain," and "Sashinō Mountain." Locally, it is referred to as "Castle Mountain" due to its association with the castle. The ''[[Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|Hitachi Fudoki]]'' mentions it as a "great mountain" located on the border between [[Niihari District (Hitachi Province)|Niihari District]] and [[Naka District (Hitachi Province)|Naka District]]. There are various theories regarding the origin of its name: * According to the tradition of {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|ja|正福寺_(笠間市)|de|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|qq=}}, the mountain's divine messengers— "white horse, white deer, and white pheasant"—led to the name "Sanpaku" (Three Whites). The Kasama City explanation states these messengers are a "white pheasant, white deer, and white fox." * The ''New Hitachi Provincial Records'' interpret the ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'' passage referring to the descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and connect it to the etymology of Sashino. It suggests "Sashinō" evolved through phonetic shifts, implying the name originated as "Sajino," which later transformed to "Sashinō." The mountain summit, separate from the former main keep, is also referred to as "Abu Mountain." The shrine once bore alternate names such as "Sanpaku Gongen" and "Abu Gongen," reflecting its historical ties to the mountain. == Deities == ;Principal deities: * {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Kami]] * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}} ;Enshrined together: * Hachiman Shrine ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|品陀和気命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|qq=}}) * Shiroyama Inari Shrine ({{ill|Uka-no-Mitama|ja|宇迦之御魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uka-no-Mitama|qq=}}) * Itsukishima Shrine ({{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵志比賣命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}) * Kagutsuchi Shrine ({{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|ja|軻遇突智神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}) * Success Inari Shrine (Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami) The shrine's deities were finalized during its relocation to the summit of Sashirō Mountain in 1873. In 1874, five shrines, including Hachiman Shrine and Shiroyama Inari Shrine, were consolidated. == Festivals == The main festival is held on November 16, along with the February 15 ''Kinen-sai'' (Prayer Festival) and the April 22 ''Rinji-sai'' (Special Festival). Sashino Shrine is managed by [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], with its events listed on their website. == References == {{Reflist|refs= The Ibaraki Prefectural Shrine Registry lists the Sashino Shrines in [[Sakuranome]] and [[Murakami]] as "Sashino," while the one in Kasama City is listed as "Sashinō." [http://www.sasiro.jp/info/index.html History of Sashirō Mountain Shōfuku-ji], retrieved September 10, 2016. [http://www.city.kasama.lg.jp/page/page006719.html History of Kasama Castle], Kasama City official website, retrieved September 10, 2016. [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ National Diet Library Digital Collections], citing ''Greater Japan Geographic Dictionary.'' Ibaraki Prefecture, [http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/seikatsukankyo/kansei/shizen/koen/09.html Kasama Prefectural Natural Park], retrieved September 10, 2016. }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:Historic sites in Kasama City]] [[Category:100 Scenic Spots of Ibaraki]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:pages that were shrank]] [[sn:Sashino Shrine (Kasama)]]}} [[ja:佐志能神社 (笠間市)]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

Former [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Village Shrine]] |Established = Unknown |MainHall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage = |Festival = November 16 |Rituals = }} '''Sashino Shrine''' (佐志能神社, ''Sashinō Jinja'') is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kasama City]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]], situated on [[Sashirō Mountain]], which includes the ruins of [[Kasama Castle]]. It is a candidate for the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} listed in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', classified as a small shrine in [[Hitachi Province]]'s [[Niihari District]]. The shrine was previously Ranked as a village shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. Due to damage caused by the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the Kasama Castle main keep area, including Sashino Shrine, is listed as off-limits on Kasama City's official website.[http://www.city.kasama.lg.jp/page/page000776.html Kasama Castle Site], Kasama City official website. However, practical access is possible, although fallen trees from Typhoon No. 19 in October 2019 have made the parking area inaccessible. == Overview == The shrine is located at the summit of Sashirō Mountain (182.1 meters), at the ruins of the main keep of Kasama Castle, designated as a tangible cultural property by Kasama City. It is part of the Kasama Prefectural Natural Park (Castle Ruins Park). The mountain is both a historical castle site and a scenic spot, included in the "100 Scenic Spots of Ibaraki" and the "100 Forest Bathing Paths of Ibaraki" under the theme "Kasama Inari and Sashirō Mountain." Sashirō Mountain has also been known as "Sanpaku Mountain," "Sajō Mountain," and "Sashinō Mountain." Locally, it is referred to as "Castle Mountain" due to its association with the castle. The ''[[Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|Hitachi Fudoki]]'' mentions it as a "great mountain" located on the border between [[Niihari District (Hitachi Province)|Niihari District]] and [[Naka District (Hitachi Province)|Naka District]]. There are various theories regarding the origin of its name: * According to the tradition of {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|ja|正福寺_(笠間市)|de|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|qq=}}, the mountain's divine messengers— "white horse, white deer, and white pheasant"—led to the name "Sanpaku" (Three Whites). The Kasama City explanation states these messengers are a "white pheasant, white deer, and white fox." * The ''New Hitachi Provincial Records'' interpret the ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'' passage referring to the descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and connect it to the etymology of Sashino. It suggests "Sashinō" evolved through phonetic shifts, implying the name originated as "Sajino," which later transformed to "Sashinō." The mountain summit, separate from the former main keep, is also referred to as "Abu Mountain." The shrine once bore alternate names such as "Sanpaku Gongen" and "Abu Gongen," reflecting its historical ties to the mountain. == Deities == ;Principal deities: * {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Kami]] * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}} ;Enshrined together: * Hachiman Shrine ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|品陀和気命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|qq=}}) * Shiroyama Inari Shrine ({{ill|Uka-no-Mitama|ja|宇迦之御魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uka-no-Mitama|qq=}}) * Itsukishima Shrine ({{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵志比賣命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}) * Kagutsuchi Shrine ({{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|ja|軻遇突智神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}) * Success Inari Shrine (Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami) The shrine's deities were finalized during its relocation to the summit of Sashirō Mountain in 1873. In 1874, five shrines, including Hachiman Shrine and Shiroyama Inari Shrine, were consolidated. == Festivals == The main festival is held on November 16, along with the February 15 ''Kinen-sai'' (Prayer Festival) and the April 22 ''Rinji-sai'' (Special Festival). Sashino Shrine is managed by [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], with its events listed on their website. == References == {{Reflist|refs= The Ibaraki Prefectural Shrine Registry lists the Sashino Shrines in [[Sakuranome]] and [[Murakami]] as "Sashino," while the one in Kasama City is listed as "Sashinō." [http://www.sasiro.jp/info/index.html History of Sashirō Mountain Shōfuku-ji], retrieved September 10, 2016. [http://www.city.kasama.lg.jp/page/page006719.html History of Kasama Castle], Kasama City official website, retrieved September 10, 2016. [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ National Diet Library Digital Collections], citing ''Greater Japan Geographic Dictionary.'' Ibaraki Prefecture, [http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/seikatsukankyo/kansei/shizen/koen/09.html Kasama Prefectural Natural Park], retrieved September 10, 2016. }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:Historic sites in Kasama City]] [[Category:100 Scenic Spots of Ibaraki]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:pages that were shrank]] [[sn:Sashino Shrine (Kasama)]]}} [[ja:佐志能神社 (笠間市)]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] == Accidentally Overwritten Content == [[Category:Pages with merged translations]][[Category:Pages with recovered content]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] [[Category:Pages with merged translations]] ==Merged second translation== {{Shrine |name = Sashino Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Sashino jinja shrine in Kasama city.jpg|280px]] |location = [[Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]], [[Kasama, Ibaraki|Kasama City]], Kasama 3613 |LatitudeDeg = 36 |LatitudeMin = 22 |LatitudeSec = 56.98 |LongitudeDeg= 140 |LongitudeMin= 16 |LongitudeSec= 2.89 |Map = Japan Ibaraki |Deities = {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}

It is a candidate for the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} listed in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', classified as a small shrine in [[Hitachi Province]]'s [[Niihari District]]. The shrine was previously Ranked as a village shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. Due to damage caused by the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the Kasama Castle main keep area, including Sashino Shrine, is listed as off-limits on Kasama City's official website.[http://www.city.kasama.lg.jp/page/page000776.html Kasama Castle Site], Kasama City official website. However, practical access is possible, although fallen trees from Typhoon No. 19 in October 2019 have made the parking area inaccessible. == Overview == The shrine is located at the summit of Sashirō Mountain (182.1 meters), at the ruins of the main keep of Kasama Castle, designated as a tangible cultural property by Kasama City. It is part of the Kasama Prefectural Natural Park (Castle Ruins Park). The mountain is both a historical castle site and a scenic spot, included in the "100 Scenic Spots of Ibaraki" and the "100 Forest Bathing Paths of Ibaraki" under the theme "Kasama Inari and Sashirō Mountain." Sashirō Mountain has also been known as "Sanpaku Mountain," "Sajō Mountain," and "Sashinō Mountain." Locally, it is referred to as "Castle Mountain" due to its association with the castle. The ''[[Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|Hitachi Fudoki]]'' mentions it as a "great mountain" located on the border between [[Niihari District (Hitachi Province)|Niihari District]] and [[Naka District (Hitachi Province)|Naka District]]. There are various theories regarding the origin of its name: * According to the tradition of {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|ja|正福寺_(笠間市)|de|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|qq=}}, the mountain's divine messengers— "white horse, white deer, and white pheasant"—led to the name "Sanpaku" (Three Whites). The Kasama City explanation states these messengers are a "white pheasant, white deer, and white fox." * The ''New Hitachi Provincial Records'' interpret the ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'' passage referring to the descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and connect it to the etymology of Sashino. It suggests "Sashinō" evolved through phonetic shifts, implying the name originated as "Sajino," which later transformed to "Sashinō." The mountain summit, separate from the former main keep, is also referred to as "Abu Mountain." The shrine once bore alternate names such as "Sanpaku Gongen" and "Abu Gongen," reflecting its historical ties to the mountain. == Deities == ;Principal deities: * {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Kami]] * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}} ;Enshrined together: * Hachiman Shrine ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|品陀和気命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|qq=}}) * Shiroyama Inari Shrine ({{ill|Uka-no-Mitama|ja|宇迦之御魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uka-no-Mitama|qq=}}) * Itsukishima Shrine ({{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵志比賣命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}) * Kagutsuchi Shrine ({{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|ja|軻遇突智神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}) * Success Inari Shrine (Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami) The shrine's deities were finalized during its relocation to the summit of Sashirō Mountain in 1873. In 1874, five shrines, including Hachiman Shrine and Shiroyama Inari Shrine, were consolidated. == Festivals == The main festival is held on November 16, along with the February 15 ''Kinen-sai'' (Prayer Festival) and the April 22 ''Rinji-sai'' (Special Festival). Sashino Shrine is managed by [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], with its events listed on their website. == References == {{Reflist|refs= The Ibaraki Prefectural Shrine Registry lists the Sashino Shrines in [[Sakuranome]] and [[Murakami]] as "Sashino," while the one in Kasama City is listed as "Sashinō." [http://www.sasiro.jp/info/index.html History of Sashirō Mountain Shōfuku-ji], retrieved September 10, 2016. [http://www.city.kasama.lg.jp/page/page006719.html History of Kasama Castle], Kasama City official website, retrieved September 10, 2016. [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ National Diet Library Digital Collections], citing ''Greater Japan Geographic Dictionary.'' Ibaraki Prefecture, [http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/seikatsukankyo/kansei/shizen/koen/09.html Kasama Prefectural Natural Park], retrieved September 10, 2016. }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:Historic sites in Kasama City]] [[Category:100 Scenic Spots of Ibaraki]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:pages that were shrank]] [[sn:Sashino Shrine (Kasama)]]}} [[ja:佐志能神社 (笠間市)]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] == Accidentally Overwritten Content == ==Merged second translation== {{Shrine |name = Sashino Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Sashino jinja shrine in Kasama city.jpg|280px]] |location = [[Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]], [[Kasama, Ibaraki|Kasama City]], Kasama 3613 |LatitudeDeg = 36 |LatitudeMin = 22 |LatitudeSec = 56.98 |LongitudeDeg= 140 |LongitudeMin= 16 |LongitudeSec= 2.89 |Map = Japan Ibaraki |Deities = {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}

Former [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Village Shrine]] |Established = Unknown |MainHall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage = |Festival = November 16 |Rituals = }} '''Sashino Shrine''' (佐志能神社, ''Sashinō Jinja'') is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kasama City]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]], situated on [[Sashirō Mountain]], which includes the ruins of [[Kasama Castle]]. It is a candidate for the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} listed in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', classified as a small shrine in [[Hitachi Province]]'s [[Niihari District]]. The shrine was previously Ranked as a village shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. Due to damage caused by the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the Kasama Castle main keep area, including Sashino Shrine, is listed as off-limits on Kasama City's official website.[http://www.city.kasama.lg.jp/page/page000776.html Kasama Castle Site], Kasama City official website. However, practical access is possible, although fallen trees from Typhoon No. 19 in October 2019 have made the parking area inaccessible. == Overview == The shrine is located at the summit of Sashirō Mountain (182.1 meters), at the ruins of the main keep of Kasama Castle, designated as a tangible cultural property by Kasama City. It is part of the Kasama Prefectural Natural Park (Castle Ruins Park). The mountain is both a historical castle site and a scenic spot, included in the "100 Scenic Spots of Ibaraki" and the "100 Forest Bathing Paths of Ibaraki" under the theme "Kasama Inari and Sashirō Mountain." Sashirō Mountain has also been known as "Sanpaku Mountain," "Sajō Mountain," and "Sashinō Mountain." Locally, it is referred to as "Castle Mountain" due to its association with the castle. The ''[[Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|Hitachi Fudoki]]'' mentions it as a "great mountain" located on the border between [[Niihari District (Hitachi Province)|Niihari District]] and [[Naka District (Hitachi Province)|Naka District]]. There are various theories regarding the origin of its name: * According to the tradition of {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|ja|正福寺_(笠間市)|de|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|qq=}}, the mountain's divine messengers— "white horse, white deer, and white pheasant"—led to the name "Sanpaku" (Three Whites). The Kasama City explanation states these messengers are a "white pheasant, white deer, and white fox." * The ''New Hitachi Provincial Records'' interpret the ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'' passage referring to the descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and connect it to the etymology of Sashino. It suggests "Sashinō" evolved through phonetic shifts, implying the name originated as "Sajino," which later transformed to "Sashinō." The mountain summit, separate from the former main keep, is also referred to as "Abu Mountain." The shrine once bore alternate names such as "Sanpaku Gongen" and "Abu Gongen," reflecting its historical ties to the mountain. == Deities == ;Principal deities: * {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Kami]] * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}} ;Enshrined together: * Hachiman Shrine ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|品陀和気命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|qq=}}) * Shiroyama Inari Shrine ({{ill|Uka-no-Mitama|ja|宇迦之御魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uka-no-Mitama|qq=}}) * Itsukishima Shrine ({{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵志比賣命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}) * Kagutsuchi Shrine ({{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|ja|軻遇突智神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}) * Success Inari Shrine (Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami) The shrine's deities were finalized during its relocation to the summit of Sashirō Mountain in 1873. In 1874, five shrines, including Hachiman Shrine and Shiroyama Inari Shrine, were consolidated. == Festivals == The main festival is held on November 16, along with the February 15 ''Kinen-sai'' (Prayer Festival) and the April 22 ''Rinji-sai'' (Special Festival). Sashino Shrine is managed by [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], with its events listed on their website. == References == {{Reflist|refs= The Ibaraki Prefectural Shrine Registry lists the Sashino Shrines in [[Sakuranome]] and [[Murakami]] as "Sashino," while the one in Kasama City is listed as "Sashinō." [http://www.sasiro.jp/info/index.html History of Sashirō Mountain Shōfuku-ji], retrieved September 10, 2016. [http://www.city.kasama.lg.jp/page/page006719.html History of Kasama Castle], Kasama City official website, retrieved September 10, 2016. [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ National Diet Library Digital Collections], citing ''Greater Japan Geographic Dictionary.'' Ibaraki Prefecture, [http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/seikatsukankyo/kansei/shizen/koen/09.html Kasama Prefectural Natural Park], retrieved September 10, 2016. }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:Historic sites in Kasama City]] [[Category:100 Scenic Spots of Ibaraki]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:pages that were shrank]] [[sn:Sashino Shrine (Kasama)]]}} [[ja:佐志能神社 (笠間市)]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] == Accidentally Overwritten Content == ==Merged second translation== {{Shrine |name = Sashino Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Sashino jinja shrine in Kasama city.jpg|280px]] |location = [[Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]], [[Kasama, Ibaraki|Kasama City]], Kasama 3613 |LatitudeDeg = 36 |LatitudeMin = 22 |LatitudeSec = 56.98 |LongitudeDeg= 140 |LongitudeMin= 16 |LongitudeSec= 2.89 |Map = Japan Ibaraki |Deities = {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}

It is a candidate for the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} listed in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', classified as a small shrine in [[Hitachi Province]]'s [[Niihari District]]. The shrine was previously Ranked as a village shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. Due to damage caused by the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the Kasama Castle main keep area, including Sashino Shrine, is listed as off-limits on Kasama City's official website.[http://www.city.kasama.lg.jp/page/page000776.html Kasama Castle Site], Kasama City official website. However, practical access is possible, although fallen trees from Typhoon No. 19 in October 2019 have made the parking area inaccessible. == Overview == The shrine is located at the summit of Sashirō Mountain (182.1 meters), at the ruins of the main keep of Kasama Castle, designated as a tangible cultural property by Kasama City. It is part of the Kasama Prefectural Natural Park (Castle Ruins Park). The mountain is both a historical castle site and a scenic spot, included in the "100 Scenic Spots of Ibaraki" and the "100 Forest Bathing Paths of Ibaraki" under the theme "Kasama Inari and Sashirō Mountain." Sashirō Mountain has also been known as "Sanpaku Mountain," "Sajō Mountain," and "Sashinō Mountain." Locally, it is referred to as "Castle Mountain" due to its association with the castle. The ''[[Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|Hitachi Fudoki]]'' mentions it as a "great mountain" located on the border between [[Niihari District (Hitachi Province)|Niihari District]] and [[Naka District (Hitachi Province)|Naka District]]. There are various theories regarding the origin of its name: * According to the tradition of {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|ja|正福寺_(笠間市)|de|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|qq=}}, the mountain's divine messengers— "white horse, white deer, and white pheasant"—led to the name "Sanpaku" (Three Whites). The Kasama City explanation states these messengers are a "white pheasant, white deer, and white fox." * The ''New Hitachi Provincial Records'' interpret the ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'' passage referring to the descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and connect it to the etymology of Sashino. It suggests "Sashinō" evolved through phonetic shifts, implying the name originated as "Sajino," which later transformed to "Sashinō." The mountain summit, separate from the former main keep, is also referred to as "Abu Mountain." The shrine once bore alternate names such as "Sanpaku Gongen" and "Abu Gongen," reflecting its historical ties to the mountain. == Deities == ;Principal deities: * {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Kami]] * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}} ;Enshrined together: * Hachiman Shrine ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|品陀和気命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|qq=}}) * Shiroyama Inari Shrine ({{ill|Uka-no-Mitama|ja|宇迦之御魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uka-no-Mitama|qq=}}) * Itsukishima Shrine ({{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵志比賣命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}) * Kagutsuchi Shrine ({{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|ja|軻遇突智神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}) * Success Inari Shrine (Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami) The shrine's deities were finalized during its relocation to the summit of Sashirō Mountain in 1873. In 1874, five shrines, including Hachiman Shrine and Shiroyama Inari Shrine, were consolidated. == Festivals == The main festival is held on November 16, along with the February 15 ''Kinen-sai'' (Prayer Festival) and the April 22 ''Rinji-sai'' (Special Festival). Sashino Shrine is managed by [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], with its events listed on their website. == References == {{Reflist|refs= The Ibaraki Prefectural Shrine Registry lists the Sashino Shrines in [[Sakuranome]] and [[Murakami]] as "Sashino," while the one in Kasama City is listed as "Sashinō." [http://www.sasiro.jp/info/index.html History of Sashirō Mountain Shōfuku-ji], retrieved September 10, 2016. [http://www.city.kasama.lg.jp/page/page006719.html History of Kasama Castle], Kasama City official website, retrieved September 10, 2016. [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ National Diet Library Digital Collections], citing ''Greater Japan Geographic Dictionary.'' Ibaraki Prefecture, [http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/seikatsukankyo/kansei/shizen/koen/09.html Kasama Prefectural Natural Park], retrieved September 10, 2016. }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:Historic sites in Kasama City]] [[Category:100 Scenic Spots of Ibaraki]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:pages that were shrank]] [[sn:Sashino Shrine (Kasama)]]}} [[ja:佐志能神社 (笠間市)]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] == Accidentally Overwritten Content == ==Merged second translation== {{Shrine |name = Sashino Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Sashino jinja shrine in Kasama city.jpg|280px]] |location = [[Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]], [[Kasama, Ibaraki|Kasama City]], Kasama 3613 |LatitudeDeg = 36 |LatitudeMin = 22 |LatitudeSec = 56.98 |LongitudeDeg= 140 |LongitudeMin= 16 |LongitudeSec= 2.89 |Map = Japan Ibaraki |Deities = {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}

However, practical access is possible, although fallen trees from Typhoon No. 19 in October 2019 have made the parking area inaccessible. == Overview == The shrine is located at the summit of Sashirō Mountain (182.1 meters), at the ruins of the main keep of Kasama Castle, designated as a tangible cultural property by Kasama City. It is part of the Kasama Prefectural Natural Park (Castle Ruins Park). The mountain is both a historical castle site and a scenic spot, included in the "100 Scenic Spots of Ibaraki" and the "100 Forest Bathing Paths of Ibaraki" under the theme "Kasama Inari and Sashirō Mountain." Sashirō Mountain has also been known as "Sanpaku Mountain," "Sajō Mountain," and "Sashinō Mountain." Locally, it is referred to as "Castle Mountain" due to its association with the castle. The ''[[Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|Hitachi Fudoki]]'' mentions it as a "great mountain" located on the border between [[Niihari District (Hitachi Province)|Niihari District]] and [[Naka District (Hitachi Province)|Naka District]]. There are various theories regarding the origin of its name: * According to the tradition of {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|ja|正福寺_(笠間市)|de|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|qq=}}, the mountain's divine messengers— "white horse, white deer, and white pheasant"—led to the name "Sanpaku" (Three Whites). The Kasama City explanation states these messengers are a "white pheasant, white deer, and white fox." * The ''New Hitachi Provincial Records'' interpret the ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'' passage referring to the descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and connect it to the etymology of Sashino. It suggests "Sashinō" evolved through phonetic shifts, implying the name originated as "Sajino," which later transformed to "Sashinō." The mountain summit, separate from the former main keep, is also referred to as "Abu Mountain." The shrine once bore alternate names such as "Sanpaku Gongen" and "Abu Gongen," reflecting its historical ties to the mountain. == Deities == ;Principal deities: * {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Kami]] * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}} ;Enshrined together: * Hachiman Shrine ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|品陀和気命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|qq=}}) * Shiroyama Inari Shrine ({{ill|Uka-no-Mitama|ja|宇迦之御魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uka-no-Mitama|qq=}}) * Itsukishima Shrine ({{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵志比賣命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}) * Kagutsuchi Shrine ({{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|ja|軻遇突智神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}) * Success Inari Shrine (Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami) The shrine's deities were finalized during its relocation to the summit of Sashirō Mountain in 1873. In 1874, five shrines, including Hachiman Shrine and Shiroyama Inari Shrine, were consolidated. == Festivals == The main festival is held on November 16, along with the February 15 ''Kinen-sai'' (Prayer Festival) and the April 22 ''Rinji-sai'' (Special Festival). Sashino Shrine is managed by [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], with its events listed on their website. == References == {{Reflist|refs= The Ibaraki Prefectural Shrine Registry lists the Sashino Shrines in [[Sakuranome]] and [[Murakami]] as "Sashino," while the one in Kasama City is listed as "Sashinō." Ibaraki Prefecture, [http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/seikatsukankyo/kansei/shizen/koen/09.html Kasama Prefectural Natural Park], retrieved September 10, 2016. }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:Historic sites in Kasama City]] [[Category:100 Scenic Spots of Ibaraki]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:pages that were shrank]] [[sn:Sashino Shrine (Kasama)]]}} [[ja:佐志能神社 (笠間市)]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] == Accidentally Overwritten Content == ==Merged second translation== {{Shrine |name = Sashino Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Sashino jinja shrine in Kasama city.jpg|280px]] |location = [[Ibaraki Prefecture|Ibaraki Prefecture]], [[Kasama, Ibaraki|Kasama City]], Kasama 3613 |LatitudeDeg = 36 |LatitudeMin = 22 |LatitudeSec = 56.98 |LongitudeDeg= 140 |LongitudeMin= 16 |LongitudeSec= 2.89 |Map = Japan Ibaraki |Deities = {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}

There are various theories regarding the origin of its name: * According to the tradition of {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|ja|正福寺_(笠間市)|de|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|qq=}}, the mountain's divine messengers— "white horse, white deer, and white pheasant"—led to the name "Sanpaku" (Three Whites). The Kasama City explanation states these messengers are a "white pheasant, white deer, and white fox." * The ''New Hitachi Provincial Records'' interpret the ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'' passage referring to the descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and connect it to the etymology of Sashino. It suggests "Sashinō" evolved through phonetic shifts, implying the name originated as "Sajino," which later transformed to "Sashinō." The mountain summit, separate from the former main keep, is also referred to as "Abu Mountain." The shrine once bore alternate names such as "Sanpaku Gongen" and "Abu Gongen," reflecting its historical ties to the mountain. == Deities == ;Principal deities: * {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Kami]] * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}} ;Enshrined together: * Hachiman Shrine ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|品陀和気命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|qq=}}) * Shiroyama Inari Shrine ({{ill|Uka-no-Mitama|ja|宇迦之御魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uka-no-Mitama|qq=}}) * Itsukishima Shrine ({{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵志比賣命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}) * Kagutsuchi Shrine ({{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|ja|軻遇突智神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}) * Success Inari Shrine (Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami) The shrine's deities were finalized during its relocation to the summit of Sashirō Mountain in 1873. In 1874, five shrines, including Hachiman Shrine and Shiroyama Inari Shrine, were consolidated. == Festivals == The main festival is held on November 16, along with the February 15 ''Kinen-sai'' (Prayer Festival) and the April 22 ''Rinji-sai'' (Special Festival). Sashino Shrine is managed by [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], with its events listed on their website. == References == {{Reflist|refs= The Ibaraki Prefectural Shrine Registry lists the Sashino Shrines in [[Sakuranome]] and [[Murakami]] as "Sashino," while the one in Kasama City is listed as "Sashinō." It is a candidate for the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} listed in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', classified as a small shrine in [[Hitachi Province]]'s [[Niihari District]]. It suggests "Sashinō" evolved through phonetic shifts, implying the name originated as "Sajino," which later transformed to "Sashinō." The mountain summit, separate from the former main keep, is also referred to as "Abu Mountain." The shrine once bore alternate names such as "Sanpaku Gongen" and "Abu Gongen," reflecting its historical ties to the mountain. == Deities == ;Principal deities: * {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Kami]] * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}} ;Enshrined together: * Hachiman Shrine ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|品陀和気命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|qq=}}) * Shiroyama Inari Shrine ({{ill|Uka-no-Mitama|ja|宇迦之御魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uka-no-Mitama|qq=}}) * Itsukishima Shrine ({{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵志比賣命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}) * Kagutsuchi Shrine ({{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|ja|軻遇突智神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}) * Success Inari Shrine (Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami) The shrine's deities were finalized during its relocation to the summit of Sashirō Mountain in 1873. In 1874, five shrines, including Hachiman Shrine and Shiroyama Inari Shrine, were consolidated. == Festivals == The main festival is held on November 16, along with the February 15 ''Kinen-sai'' (Prayer Festival) and the April 22 ''Rinji-sai'' (Special Festival). Sashino Shrine is managed by [[Kasama Inari Shrine]], with its events listed on their website. == References == {{Reflist|refs= The Ibaraki Prefectural Shrine Registry lists the Sashino Shrines in [[Sakuranome]] and [[Murakami]] as "Sashino," while the one in Kasama City is listed as "Sashinō." Former [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Village Shrine]] |Established = Unknown |MainHall = |Alias = |Pilgrimage = |Festival = November 16 |Rituals = }} '''Sashino Shrine''' (佐志能神社, ''Sashinō Jinja'') is a Shinto shrine located in [[Kasama City]], [[Ibaraki Prefecture]], situated on [[Sashirō Mountain]], which includes the ruins of [[Kasama Castle]]. It is a candidate for the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} listed in the ''[[Engishiki]]'', classified as a small shrine in [[Hitachi Province]]'s [[Niihari District]]. The shrine was previously Ranked as a village shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. Due to damage caused by the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]], the Kasama Castle main keep area, including Sashino Shrine, is listed as off-limits on Kasama City's official website.[http://www.city.kasama.lg.jp/page/page000776.html Kasama Castle Site], Kasama City official website. However, practical access is possible, although fallen trees from Typhoon No. 19 in October 2019 have made the parking area inaccessible. == Overview == The shrine is located at the summit of Sashirō Mountain (182.1 meters), at the ruins of the main keep of Kasama Castle, designated as a tangible cultural property by Kasama City. It is part of the Kasama Prefectural Natural Park (Castle Ruins Park). The mountain is both a historical castle site and a scenic spot, included in the "100 Scenic Spots of Ibaraki" and the "100 Forest Bathing Paths of Ibaraki" under the theme "Kasama Inari and Sashirō Mountain." Sashirō Mountain has also been known as "Sanpaku Mountain," "Sajō Mountain," and "Sashinō Mountain." Locally, it is referred to as "Castle Mountain" due to its association with the castle. The ''[[Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|Hitachi Fudoki]]'' mentions it as a "great mountain" located on the border between [[Niihari District (Hitachi Province)|Niihari District]] and [[Naka District (Hitachi Province)|Naka District]]. There are various theories regarding the origin of its name: * According to the tradition of {{ill|Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|ja|正福寺_(笠間市)|de|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōfuku-ji (Kasama)|qq=}}, the mountain's divine messengers— "white horse, white deer, and white pheasant"—led to the name "Sanpaku" (Three Whites). The Kasama City explanation states these messengers are a "white pheasant, white deer, and white fox." * The ''New Hitachi Provincial Records'' interpret the ''Shinsen Shōjiroku'' passage referring to the descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and connect it to the etymology of Sashino. It suggests "Sashinō" evolved through phonetic shifts, implying the name originated as "Sajino," which later transformed to "Sashinō." The mountain summit, separate from the former main keep, is also referred to as "Abu Mountain." The shrine once bore alternate names such as "Sanpaku Gongen" and "Abu Gongen," reflecting its historical ties to the mountain. == Deities == ;Principal deities: * {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Kami]] * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}} ;Enshrined together: * Hachiman Shrine ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|ja|品陀和気命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|qq=}}) * Shiroyama Inari Shrine ({{ill|Uka-no-Mitama|ja|宇迦之御魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uka-no-Mitama|qq=}}) * Itsukishima Shrine ({{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵志比賣命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|qq=}}) * Kagutsuchi Shrine ({{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|ja|軻遇突智神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}) * Success Inari Shrine (Uka-no-Mitama-no-Kami) The shrine's deities were finalized during its relocation to the summit of Sashirō Mountain in 1873.
